answer:
**Iqbal's "Bachay ki Dua" (ch. 16 of *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*) presents a child's voice in direct prayer to God, aspiring to selfless ideals: becoming a candle dispelling worldly darkness, a flower adorning the homeland, a moth drawn to the flame of knowledge, and a champion of the poor and weak.**[^1] The prayer emphasizes communal and spiritual service over personal ties:

> May my life's spark dispel the darkness of the world,  
> May my shining light in every place be unfurled.  
> May I become my homeland's grace and pride,  
> Like a flower that adorns the garden-side.[^2]

**In contrast, "Maa ka Khwab" (ch. 29) depicts a dream-dialogue where parental (mother's) and child voices explicitly clash.** The mother, anguished by her son's absence among lamp-bearing boys (symbolizing enlightenment or mission), reproaches him for neglect:

> I knew him, and I cried, "My life!  
> Where have you come, and left me all alone?  
> Your absence leaves me in deep turmoil;  
> I string each day a garland of my tears.  
> You showed no care for me at all,  
> and left. A fine devotion to recall!"[^3]

**The child counters that her tears have extinguished his lamp—his inner light or purpose—highlighting how parental grief hinders the child's path:** 

> "My absence makes you weep and cry,  
> but in that, no good for me can lie."  
> [...]  
> "Do you now see what has become of it?  
> It is your tears that have extinguished it."[^4]

**The corpus contains no explicit commentary linking or contrasting these poems, nor any analysis of "voices speaking past each other."** The child in ch. 16 embodies unencumbered aspiration; in ch. 29, the voices collide over dependence (mother's emotional claim) versus mission (child's light), with the child prioritizing higher purpose despite (or because of) parental sorrow. No other works in the corpus thematically bridge parental-child miscommunication in this Iqbal pairing.[^5]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 16: "Bachay Ki Dua" ([full chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/16-bachay-ki-dua/translation/)).
[^2]: Ibid., [¶p-4bb8de–p-01b332](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/16-bachay-ki-dua/translation/#p-4bb8de).
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 29: "Maa ka Khwab" ([full chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/29-maa-ka-khwab/translation/)), [¶p-9c5ea5–p-4c138e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/29-maa-ka-khwab/translation/#p-9c5ea5).
[^4]: Ibid., [¶p-508d74–p-4feffc](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/29-maa-ka-khwab/translation/#p-508d74).
[^5]: See TF-IDF related chapters for each: ch. 16 links to Iqbal's "Falsafa-e-Gham" (Part 3, ch. 7) and Ghalib; ch. 29 to Ghalib's ghazal on a quenched lamp (ch. 171). No parent-child voice analysis across poems.
